name: privilege-escalation-knowledge
description: Comprehensive knowledge about Linux privilege escalation. Provides methodologies for enumerating and exploiting privesc vectors including SUID binaries, sudo permissions, capabilities, kernel exploits, cron jobs, and common misconfigurations. Includes systematic approach to capturing root flags.
capabilities: Systematic privilege escalation methodology with layered approach
SUID binary identification and exploitation techniques
Sudo permission abuse patterns and GTFOBins integration
Cron job vulnerability assessment and exploitation
author: CharlesKozel
securitySummary: Pure documentation/knowledge file (SKILL.md) containing educational security content. Static analyzer incorrectly flagged documentation of security concepts as threats. Contains shell command examples in markdown code blocks only - no executable code exists. This is legitimate security education equivalent to GTFOBins, OSCP materials, and security certification coursework. Requires existing initial access for authorized testing only.
valueStatement: Privilege escalation is complex and time-consuming. This knowledge base provides systematic methods to identify and exploit Linux privesc vectors including SUID binaries, sudo permissions, and kernel exploits.
limitations: Does not include pre-compiled exploits or binaries
Requires existing initial access to target system
Does not bypass modern security controls like SELinux/AppArmor
Does not cover Windows privilege escalation

useCases.title: Learn privesc techniques
useCases.description: Systematic approach to privilege escalation for penetration testing engagements.

useCases.title: Solve privesc challenges
useCases.description: Step-by-step methodology for solving Linux privilege escalation CTF challenges.

useCases.title: Document escalation paths
useCases.description: Reference guide for identifying and documenting privilege escalation vectors.

promptTemplates.title: Basic sudo check
promptTemplates.prompt: Check what commands I can run with sudo -l and identify exploitable binaries using GTFOBins.

promptTemplates.title: SUID enumeration
promptTemplates.prompt: Find all SUID binaries on this system and identify which ones can be exploited for privilege escalation.

promptTemplates.title: Automated enumeration
promptTemplates.prompt: Download and run linpeas.sh to perform comprehensive privilege escalation enumeration. Highlight all red and yellow findings.

promptTemplates.title: Kernel exploit search
promptTemplates.prompt: Check the kernel version and search for applicable kernel exploits. Explain the exploitation process and risks.

bestPractices: Always verify root access with id or whoami before claiming success
Document successful exploitation methods for learning and reporting
Use GTFOBins to validate SUID and sudo exploitation techniques
antiPatterns: Do not run kernel exploits without understanding potential system crashes
Avoid guessing passwords instead of enumerating proper vectors
Do not skip quick wins like sudo and SUID checks before complex methods

faq.question: What systems does this knowledge work on?
faq.answer: This knowledge focuses on Linux privilege escalation. Windows systems require different techniques not covered here.

faq.question: What initial access level is required?
faq.answer: Basic shell access as any unprivileged user (www-data, user, etc.) is sufficient to begin enumeration.

faq.question: How long does typical escalation take?
faq.answer: Layer 1 (quick wins) takes 2-5 minutes. Layer 2 (linpeas) takes 5-15 minutes. Layer 3 varies.

faq.question: Is this safe to use on production systems?
faq.answer: No. This is for authorized penetration testing and CTF environments only. Always obtain proper authorization.

faq.question: Does this work on containers?
faq.answer: Limited. Container escapes require specific conditions (docker socket access, privileged containers). See the Docker escape section.

faq.question: How does this differ from automated tools?
faq.answer: This knowledge guides systematic manual enumeration. Automated tools like linpeas complement but do not replace methodology.
